title: Pulse-Driven Reconfiguration of Fractional Polar Topology in Zr-Substituted Barium Titanate
abstract: Polar topological textures in ferroelectrics can host internal structure beyond a single integer topological charge. Here, effective-Hamiltonian molecular-dynamics simulations are used to examine whether such internal fractional topology can be reconfigured by local electric excitation in ordered 12.5% Zr-substituted barium titanate. Chemical doubling along the polar axis stabilizes a coupled nanodomain texture consisting of alternating Q = -2 antiskyrmionic and Q = +4 skyrmionic slices, in which the local topological charge fragments into six -1/3 and six +2/3 localized contributions, denoted here as topological quarks, separated by Bloch-point-like singular conversion regions. Picosecond local electric-field pulses applied to selected vortex-core columns drive reconfiguration of the internal dipolar texture of a 2.6 nm nanodomain. Under a binary pulse-mask protocol addressing the six vortex cores, all 64 masks lead, within the chosen low-temperature simulation protocol, to distinct relaxed metastable configurations. The switching calculations are performed in a cryogenic regime, and the programmed states remain stable over at least 1 ns of field-free evolution on the simulation timescale. The resulting configurations are distinguishable both by sector-resolved topological fingerprints and by their real-space polarization fields. These results provide a computational proof of concept that fractional polar topology in a ferroelectric nanodomain can be locally reconfigured by ultrafast electric excitation and used as a multistate configurational degree of freedom in an idealized low-temperature setting.
summary: This paper demonstrates through simulations that ultrafast electric pulses can reconfigure the fractional topological charge within a ferroelectric nanodomain. By applying specific pulse patterns to vortex cores, researchers can program the system into 64 distinct, stable, and distinguishable metastable states. This provides a computational foundation for using topological textures as a multi-state degree of freedom in future nano-electronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating whether the internal fractional polar topology of a ferroelectric nanodomain can be actively and locally reconfigured using ultrafast electric-field pulses to serve as a programmable multistate degree of freedom.
main_result: All 64 possible pulse-mask configurations lead to distinct, stable, and distinguishable metastable topological configurations that persist for at least 1 ns in a cryogenic regime.
method: Effective-Hamiltonian molecular-dynamics (EHMD) simulations using a first-principles-parameterized framework and a binary pulse-mask protocol.
model_or_system: An ordered 12.5% Zr-substituted barium titanate (BT) nanodomain featuring a coupled Q = -2 antiskyrmionic and Q = +4 skyrmionic texture.
key_observables: Sector-resolved topological fingerprints, real-space polarization fields, local topological charge density, and pairwise mean angular mismatch.
important_parameters: Cryogenic temperature (10 K), picosecond pulse duration (0.45 ps), electric field amplitude (6 MV/cm), and nanodomain diameter (2.6 nm).
main_assumptions: The study is a computational proof of concept performed in an idealized cryogenic setting and uses a simplified effective Hamiltonian approach rather than full atomistic complexity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the nanodomain structure and topological quarks; Figure 3 displays topological charge fingerprints and inter-slice charge transfer; Figure 4 presents matrices of angular mismatch and RMS vector difference to demonstrate state distinguishability; Supplemental Figure S1 shows the potential-energy surface and switching path.
paper_structure: The paper defines the topological nanodomain structure, describes the pulse-driven switching mechanism and simulation protocol, quantifies the resulting 64 metastable states via topological and polarization metrics, analyzes the energetic barriers and thermal stability, and concludes with a proof of concept for topological reconfiguration.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Reference Q=−2/Q=+4 nanodomain texture and pulse-addressing protocol. (a) (111)-plane projection of the lower half of the doubled unit cell (z1), showing the Q=−2 antiskyrmionic slice with six localized −1/3 topological quarks. Orange vectors indicate the vortex-core dipoles. (b) Corresponding projection of the upper half (z2), showing the Q=+4 skyrmionic slice with six localized +2/3 topological quarks. Circled regions indicate Bloch-point-like singular conversion motifs. (c) Labeling of the six vortex cores used to define the binary pulse mask, together with a schematic of the tangential local excitation field. (d) Temporal profile of the electric-field pulse used for...

Figure 2. Representative pulse-induced reconfiguration of the coupled topological nanodomain. Relaxed polarization fields and local topological charge densities in the two inequivalent (111) slices after application of selected pulse masks. Left and right panels show the lower (z1) and upper (z2) halves of the doubled unit cell, respectively. (a) Pulse mask 100000: excitation of vortex core 1. (b) Pulse mask 011000: simultaneous excitation of vortex cores 2 and 3. (c) Pulse mask 101010: simultaneous excitation of three alternating vortex cores. (d) Pulse mask 111111: complete inversion of the stacked Q=−2/Q=+4 texture into Q=+4/Q=−2.

Figure 3. Topological fingerprints of the full 26=64 pulse-mask set. (a) Sector-integrated local topological charges for all 64 pulse masks, including the 000000-reference state. The lower and upper (111) slices are represented by 𝑞1 (𝑛) and 𝑞2 (𝑛), respectively, with n=1, …,6. (b) Slice-integrated topological charges of the lower and upper halves of the doubled unit cell for all pulse masks. (c) Inter-slice sector-wise charge difference, 𝛥𝑞𝑛= 𝑞2 (𝑛) −𝑞1 (𝑛), for corresponding vortex positions. (d) Minimum 𝐿∞ distance of each state to its nearest neighbor among the 64 programmed states, demonstrating topological distinguishability of all programmed configurations.

Figure 4. Polarization-field distinguishability of the programmed states. All comparisons are based on time-averaged polarization fields obtained from the final 200 ps of the field-free relaxation and restricted to a cylindrical mask centered on the nanodomain axis. (a) Pairwise mean angular mismatch between all 64 programmed states. (b) Pairwise relative RMS vector difference between all 64 programmed states. (c) Minimum off-diagonal mean angular mismatch and minimum off- diagonal relative RMS vector difference for each state, quantifying worst-case structural distinguishability in polarization- field space.
